home.hero_title: Sirviendo a Ranchita construyendo unidad, promoviendo resiliencia y empoderando las voces de la comunidad
home.hero_subtitle: La Organización de Servicio Comunitario Voluntario del Valle de Montezuma ha servido a Ranchita desde 1973.
home.mission_text: MVVCSO existe para fortalecer la comunidad de Ranchita a través de la preparación para emergencias, seguridad alimentaria, participación juvenil, apoyo a personas mayores y voluntariado entre vecinos. Somos 100% voluntarios y hemos servido a esta comunidad rural desde 1973.
home.newsletter_subtitle: Recibe el Informe de Resiliencia de Ranchita — noticias, eventos y recursos comunitarios.
home.donate_cta_text: Cada dólar construye un Ranchita más fuerte. Tu donación financia preparación para emergencias, seguridad alimentaria, programas juveniles y más.

about.title: Nuestra Historia
about.subtitle: De bomberos voluntarios a campeones comunitarios — más de 50 años de servicio en el campo.
about.board_subtitle: Siete miembros dedicados de la comunidad guiando a MVVCSO hacia su próximo capítulo.
about.transparency_text: MVVCSO está comprometido con la apertura y la responsabilidad. Las actas de las reuniones de la junta, los informes financieros y los documentos organizacionales se publicarán aquí a medida que estén disponibles.

programs.title: Nuestros Programas
programs.subtitle: Fortaleciendo Ranchita a través del servicio comunitario, la preparación para emergencias y el apoyo entre vecinos.
programs.cert_description: Entrenamiento del Equipo de Respuesta a Emergencias Comunitarias para preparar a los residentes para desastres y emergencias.
programs.food_description: Asociación con Feeding San Diego y facilitación de inscripción en CalFresh para residentes necesitados.
programs.senior_description: Programas y servicios que apoyan a los residentes mayores de Ranchita con salud, seguridad y conexión.
programs.youth_description: El Consejo Juvenil de Ranchita brinda liderazgo, mentoría y oportunidades educativas para jóvenes residentes.
programs.emergency_description: Programas de preparación para desastres, planes de comunicación y talleres de resiliencia para el campo.
programs.volunteer_description: Coordinando esfuerzos voluntarios en todos los programas. Más de 50 años de vecinos ayudando a vecinos.
programs.cta_text: Siempre buscamos voluntarios dedicados para ayudar a servir a la comunidad de Ranchita.

blog.title: Blog Comunitario
blog.subtitle: Noticias, historias y recursos de la comunidad de Ranchita.

donate.title: Apoya a Ranchita
donate.subtitle: Cada dólar construye una comunidad más fuerte. MVVCSO es una organización sin fines de lucro 501(c)(3) — tu donación es deducible de impuestos.
donate.impact_25: $25 = Kit de suministros de emergencia para una familia
donate.impact_50: $50 = Alimenta a 10 familias a través de nuestra despensa
donate.impact_250: $250 = Apoya el programa juvenil por un mes
donate.tax_notice: MVVCSO es una organización sin fines de lucro 501(c)(3). Tu donación es deducible de impuestos en toda la extensión de la ley.
donate.coming_soon: El procesamiento de donaciones estará disponible pronto. Mientras tanto, puedes donar a través de nuestra página de Zeffy.
donate.success_text: Tu generosa donación ayuda a fortalecer la comunidad de Ranchita. Recibirás un recibo por correo electrónico.

contact.title: Contáctanos
contact.subtitle: Nos encantaría saber de ti. Comunícate con preguntas, ideas o para participar.
contact.error: Algo salió mal. Inténtalo de nuevo o escríbenos directamente.

pct.title: Caminata Virtual PCT
pct.subtitle: Explora el Sendero de la Cresta del Pacífico a través del patio trasero de Ranchita — el corazón del campo de San Diego.
pct.community_text: El Sendero de la Cresta del Pacífico pasa por nuestra comunidad. Ranchita ha servido como punto de reabastecimiento para excursionistas durante décadas, ofreciendo agua, descanso y cálidas bienvenidas a viajeros cansados.

meta.title: __TODO_ES__ Wildfire Preparedness for Ranchita
meta.description: __TODO_ES__ Backcountry-tailored wildfire preparedness for Ranchita and the Montezuma Valley. Built on CAL FIRE's Ready for Wildfire program and San Diego County's Ready, Set, Go! framework, with Ranchita-specific guidance for defensible space, animals and livestock, evacuation, and mutual aid.

hero.title: __TODO_ES__ Wildfire Preparedness
hero.subtitle: __TODO_ES__ What we do here in the backcountry — built on CAL FIRE and San Diego County guidance, layered with the realities of Ranchita.

why.heading: __TODO_ES__ Why this matters in Ranchita
why.para_1: __TODO_ES__ Ranchita sits at the summit of S22, surrounded by chaparral and pine-oak fuels that ember-drive fires across long distances under Santa Ana wind conditions. The threat is year-round, not just summer and fall, and ember storms can ignite homes well beyond the visible fire line.
why.para_2: __TODO_ES__ Professional firefighting response is volunteer-supported and farther away than in town. That's not a problem to fix — it's the geography we live in. It does mean that the most consequential first response in the first minutes of any fire is almost always residents who are ready, calm, and clear about what to do.

ready_set_go.tip_body: __TODO_ES__ Ready–Set–Go is the official wildfire-action framework used by CAL FIRE and San Diego County Fire. The four sections below extend it with Ranchita-specific context — what defensible space looks like on chaparral parcels, how to plan for multi-animal households, which routes lead out of the valley, and how neighbors support firefighters without becoming obstacles.

prepare.title: __TODO_ES__ Prepare
prepare.description: __TODO_ES__ Defensible space, home hardening, rural-property specifics, and seasonal maintenance.

intro.lead: __TODO_ES__ Defensible space and home hardening together are the most consequential things a Ranchita property owner can do before a fire. They are also the things that no fire engine can do for you on the day of.

zones.heading: __TODO_ES__ Defensible space — the three zones
zones.lead: __TODO_ES__ California's defensible-space framework divides the area around a structure into concentric zones, each with progressively less restrictive maintenance requirements as distance from the home increases. The distances are measured from the exterior walls of the home (or from the edge of attached features like decks). On slopes, recommended distances increase because fire travels faster uphill.
zones.diagram_label_zone1: __TODO_ES__ Zone 1 — 5 to 30 ft (50 ft in San Diego County)
zones.diagram_label_zone2: __TODO_ES__ Zone 2 — 30 to 100 ft (50 to 100 ft in San Diego County)
zones.diagram_aria: __TODO_ES__ Top-down diagram of defensible-space zones around a house: Zone 0 immediately around the structure, Zone 1 extending outward, Zone 2 extending further outward.
zones.sd_county_note: __TODO_ES__ San Diego County requires 50 ft of clearance in Zone 1, stricter than the state minimum.
zones.slope_note: __TODO_ES__ On slopes, extend Zone 1 and Zone 2 distances. Steeper slope = more extension.

zone0.heading: __TODO_ES__ Zone 0 — the ember-resistant zone (0 to 5 ft)
zone0.regulatory_note: __TODO_ES__ Zone 0 was created by Assembly Bill 3074 (2020) and updated by Senate Bill 504 (2024). The state Board of Forestry and Fire Protection completed rulemaking in late 2025 under Governor Newsom's Executive Order N-18-25. Zone 0 is currently a strongly recommended practice with regulatory adoption progressing — apply it now whether or not enforcement has reached your parcel.
zone0.body: __TODO_ES__ Replace bark mulch and combustible landscaping in the immediate 5 ft around the home with hardscape (gravel, pavers, concrete). Remove all dead and dying plants. Clear leaves and needles from roof, gutters, deck, porch, and stairs. Trim branches at least 10 ft from chimneys and stovepipes. Remove combustible furniture, planters, doormats, and stored firewood from within 5 ft of the home. The first 5 ft of any deck attached to the house is part of Zone 0 — keep it clear.

zone1.heading: __TODO_ES__ Zone 1 — lean, clean, and green (5 to 30 ft, or 5 to 50 ft in San Diego County)
zone1.required_action: __TODO_ES__ San Diego County requires 50 ft of clearance in Zone 1, stricter than the state minimum. Plan to 50 ft.
zone1.body: __TODO_ES__ Remove dead vegetation. Trim tree branches at least 6 ft above the ground. Maintain vertical spacing between shrubs and trees — a useful rule of thumb is shrub height multiplied by three for clearance to the lowest tree branch. Maintain horizontal spacing between shrubs and trees, with more space required on steeper slopes and for taller vegetation. Do not stack firewood against the house. Outbuildings and propane tanks need 10 ft of bare mineral soil clearance and an additional 10 ft of no-flammable-vegetation perimeter beyond that.

zone2.heading: __TODO_ES__ Zone 2 — fuel reduction (30 to 100 ft, or 50 to 100 ft in San Diego County)
zone2.body: __TODO_ES__ Mow annual grass to a maximum height of 4 inches. Remove fallen leaves, needles, twigs, bark, cones, and small branches — depth of 3 inches is the maximum acceptable. Thin overgrown shrubs and trees so that crowns do not interlock. Adjust distances upward where slope amplifies fire behavior.

slope.heading: __TODO_ES__ Slope adjustments
slope.body: __TODO_ES__ Fire travels faster uphill. On slopes, extend Zone 1 and Zone 2 distances proportionally. Public Resources Code § 4291 sets the framework; San Diego County's stricter Zone 1 layers on top of it. Steeper slopes need more clearance — both up-slope and down-slope of the home.

hardening.heading: __TODO_ES__ Home hardening — making the structure ember-resistant
hardening.lead: __TODO_ES__ Most homes lost to wildfire are not ignited by direct flame contact — they are ignited by wind-driven embers that arrive ahead of the fire front and find combustible materials on or near the structure. Hardening closes those entry points. The 2026 California Wildland-Urban Interface Code (CWUIC, Title 24 Part 7), which absorbed the previous Chapter 7A, sets the current performance standards.

roof.heading: __TODO_ES__ Roof
roof.body: __TODO_ES__ Class A fire rating is required in fire hazard severity zones. Acceptable Class A coverings include asphalt fiberglass composition shingles, clay or cement tile, and metal panels. Untreated wood shake is the highest-risk roof type in chaparral country. Keep the roof clear of pine needles, leaves, and other debris. Install a metal drip edge that fully covers the space above the gutter.

vents.heading: __TODO_ES__ Vents
vents.body: __TODO_ES__ Embers entering attic vents is one of the most common ignition paths. Replace any 1/4-inch mesh — too coarse to block embers. Install non-combustible corrosion-resistant metal mesh between 1/16 and 1/8 inch. Avoid fiberglass or plastic mesh — both melt under heat. The upgrade path is California State Fire Marshal-listed ember-resistant WUI vents.

eaves.heading: __TODO_ES__ Eaves and soffits
eaves.body: __TODO_ES__ Enclose with non-combustible or ignition-resistant material. Plug or caulk gaps around rafter tails. On patio covers, box in eaves using the same materials as the roof.

windows.heading: __TODO_ES__ Windows
windows.body: __TODO_ES__ Install dual-paned windows with at least one tempered-glass layer — radiant heat alone can crack single-pane glass before flame contact, allowing embers in. Limit the number and size of windows facing large vegetation areas. Add screens to operable windows.

siding.heading: __TODO_ES__ Siding
siding.body: __TODO_ES__ Stucco, fiber cement, or treated wood are the preferred materials. Extend protective siding from foundation to roofline. Repair gaps, holes, and rot — any opening is an ember entry point.

decks.heading: __TODO_ES__ Decks and patios
decks.body: __TODO_ES__ Use non-combustible or ignition-resistant deck boards. Clear the under-deck area of debris and combustibles. The first 5 ft of any deck adjacent to the house is part of Zone 0 — no combustible furniture, planters, or stored items there.

chimneys.heading: __TODO_ES__ Chimneys
chimneys.body: __TODO_ES__ Install a spark arrestor screen with openings between 3/8 inch and 1/2 inch. Close the fireplace flue during fire season when not in use.

garage.heading: __TODO_ES__ Garage doors
garage.body: __TODO_ES__ Apply weather stripping around and under the door to block embers. Equip the door with a battery backup so it operates during a public safety power shutoff or a grid loss.

fences.heading: __TODO_ES__ Fences
fences.body: __TODO_ES__ Use non-combustible material for the section of fence that connects to the house. Wood fences ignite easily and conduct fire directly to siding and eaves.

rural.heading: __TODO_ES__ Rural-property specifics
rural.lead: __TODO_ES__ Ranchita parcels include features urban defensible-space guides do not cover: outbuildings, propane and fuel tanks, wells and water tanks, long driveways, and solar arrays.

outbuildings.heading: __TODO_ES__ Outbuildings
outbuildings.body: __TODO_ES__ Keep 10 ft of clearance to bare mineral soil around sheds, barns, and workshops, plus an additional 10 ft of no-flammable-vegetation perimeter beyond that. Apply the same hardening principles where feasible — Class A roofs, ember-resistant vents. Outbuildings too close to a home become an ember source if they ignite.

propane.heading: __TODO_ES__ Propane and fuel tanks
propane.body: __TODO_ES__ 10 ft of clearance to bare mineral soil. Keep dry vegetation, woodpiles, and combustibles outside that ring. Mark tanks for fire crews — emergency responders need to identify the location and shut-off valve placement quickly.

water.heading: __TODO_ES__ Wells and water sources
water.body: __TODO_ES__ Mark well housing for fire-crew visibility. If you have a water storage tank, pool, or large trough that crews could draft from in a structure-defense action, install a clear marker visible from the driveway. Keep the approach to any water source clear of debris and obstacles. A 1,500-gallon tank with a hose connection is a significant defense asset.

driveway.heading: __TODO_ES__ Driveways and access
driveway.body: __TODO_ES__ Keep 10 ft of horizontal clearance from each edge of the driveway and 14 ft of overhead clearance for fire engines. Type 3 engines need approximately 10 to 12 ft of width. Gates that swing inward are fastest for crews; better still, have gates that can be removed quickly. Address numbers must be visible from the road, large and reflective.

solar.heading: __TODO_ES__ Solar arrays
solar.body: __TODO_ES__ Keep the array surroundings clear of vegetation. Inverter and battery enclosures benefit from the same hardening — non-combustible siding, ember-resistant vents.

chaparral.heading: __TODO_ES__ Chaparral-specific considerations
chaparral.tip_body: __TODO_ES__ Chaparral is one of the most ember-productive fuel types in California. Ranchita's dominant fuels — chamise, ceanothus, manzanita, and sage — appear on every CAL FIRE high-flammability list.
chaparral.body: __TODO_ES__ Ember storms can travel a mile or more downwind ahead of a fire front. Ember showers ignite structures via vents, accumulated debris, and combustible landscaping long before flame arrives. In drought conditions, fuel moisture stays low year-round — Santa Ana wind events from late summer through winter are the highest-risk periods, but a wind event in any season can produce extreme fire behavior. The 2025 Fire Hazard Severity Zone map update placed virtually the entire San Diego County backcountry in the Very High designation.

calendar.heading: __TODO_ES__ Seasonal maintenance calendar
calendar.lead: __TODO_ES__ Defensible space is not a one-time project. The four-window rhythm below keeps a property in compliance year-round.
calendar.preseason_tasks: __TODO_ES__ Roof inspection. Gutter cleaning. Tree-branch trim — 6 ft above the ground, 10 ft from chimneys. Brush clearance to 50-ft Zone 1. Vent mesh inspection. Propane and outbuilding clearance refresh. Driveway access clearance. Address visibility check.
calendar.season_tasks: __TODO_ES__ Mow annual grass to 4-inch maximum — repeat as it regrows. Daily roof and gutter check during Santa Ana wind events. Test water sources. Confirm Genasys Protect zone code on phones. Confirm trailer is hitched and ready if you have animals.
calendar.postseason_tasks: __TODO_ES__ Post-rain debris removal. Fence repair. Replanting plan — choose fire-resistant species. Equipment maintenance.
calendar.quarterly_tasks: __TODO_ES__ Animal evacuation trailer practice. Water-source signage check. Emergency contact list refresh on phone.

checklist.heading: __TODO_ES__ Printable inspection checklist
checklist.lead: __TODO_ES__ Use this as a quarterly walk-around. The browser print function produces a clean printable version.
checklist.zone1_heading: __TODO_ES__ Zone 1 (5 to 30 ft, or 5 to 50 ft in San Diego County)
checklist.zone2_heading: __TODO_ES__ Zone 2 (30 to 100 ft, or 50 to 100 ft in San Diego County)

animals.title: __TODO_ES__ Animals & Livestock
animals.description: __TODO_ES__ Standalone, comprehensive coverage for pets and livestock — multi-dog households, horses, goats, chickens, evacuation kits, destinations, and shelter-in-place protocols.

intro.tip_body: __TODO_ES__ Multi-dog households are common here — four to six dogs per property is normal — and most parcels have at least some livestock. The closest large-animal evacuation site is roughly 30 miles away in Lakeside. Trailer drives are long. Animal evacuation cannot be improvised. By the time you have the order, the trailer is hitched, the carriers are assembled, and you know exactly which animals go in which vehicle.

principle.heading: __TODO_ES__ Prepare, don't improvise
principle.body: __TODO_ES__ Every animal preparedness step in this guide assumes one principle: the work happens before the warning, not after. A horse that has not been loaded into a trailer in calm conditions cannot be expected to load calmly in smoke. A go-kit assembled during evacuation is an incomplete go-kit. Practice the steps when there is no pressure.

multipet.heading: __TODO_ES__ Multi-pet households (small animals)
multipet.body: __TODO_ES__ Maintain at least one carrier per animal, pre-assembled and labeled with the animal's name, your name, and your phone number. Keep collars on with current ID tags, microchip records up to date, and printed photos of each animal in your go-kit. Have a pre-positioned go-kit (see below). If you cannot move every animal in one trip, decide in advance which animals go first — most vulnerable or most dependent typically lead. If you have multiple drivers in the household, plan multi-vehicle evacuation.

kit.heading: __TODO_ES__ Pet evacuation kit
kit.lead: __TODO_ES__ Per pet, plan for a 7-day supply. The kit lives in a single labeled tote in a known-accessible location during fire season.
kit.food_body: __TODO_ES__ Seven days of dry food (or specialty diet) in a sealed container. Manual can opener if applicable. Two bowls per animal — food and water.
kit.water_body: __TODO_ES__ Approximately one gallon per medium dog per day, half a gallon per cat, scaling up for larger animals. Plan three days minimum, seven preferred.
kit.meds_body: __TODO_ES__ One-week supply of any prescriptions, plus written prescriptions in a waterproof bag so you can refill at a destination pharmacy. Vet's contact info on the outside of the bag.
kit.records_body: __TODO_ES__ Vaccination certificates, microchip numbers, recent photos (refresh annually), owner contact on collar and inside collar pocket, adoption papers or proof of ownership.
kit.comfort_body: __TODO_ES__ One item with home scent (familiar bedding), one favorite toy. Fitted leash or harness, ready to grab.
kit.firstaid_body: __TODO_ES__ Pet first aid kit — gauze, vet wrap, saline rinse, tweezers, styptic powder. Add species-specific items per your veterinarian's guidance.
kit.transport_body: __TODO_ES__ One labeled carrier per animal. Label includes owner name, owner phone, animal name, and any health note.

horses.heading: __TODO_ES__ Horses
horses.body: __TODO_ES__ Practice trailer loading quarterly at minimum. A horse that won't load calmly under stress cannot be expected to load calmly in smoke and confusion. Store halters and leads in a known-accessible location, not scattered through the barn. Plan water at 8 to 12 gallons per horse per day at rest, up to 20 to 25 gallons in hot weather or with heavy work, and 15 to 20 gallons for pregnant or lactating mares. Bring some hay — destinations may run short, especially in the first 24 hours of a major fire. Apply identification you can verify in a chaotic shelter: leg bands, livestock paint marks, or halter tags with your name and phone. Pack a basic hoof care kit and any weather protection your horse normally uses.

small_livestock.heading: __TODO_ES__ Goats, sheep, and llamas
small_livestock.body: __TODO_ES__ Plan water at 2 to 4 gallons per goat or sheep per day, 3 to 5 for llamas. Hot weather increases consumption by 30 to 50 percent. Goats are less docile loaders than horses — practice repeatedly with treats, then short rides, then practice with neighbors' animals so they get used to herd transport. Pasture animals follow each other in motion; lead the dominant animal first.

poultry.heading: __TODO_ES__ Chickens, ducks, and other poultry
poultry.body: __TODO_ES__ Catching during stress is the hardest part. Have a catch net and an enclosed catch area — closing the coop door at night gives you a manageable dawn evacuation. Transport in cardboard pet carriers, cat carriers, or labeled poultry crates: one bird per medium carrier, two or three per large. Plan water at roughly one gallon per eleven birds per day. Poultry overheat fast in trailers — park in shade if you stop, ventilation is critical.

pigs.heading: __TODO_ES__ Pigs
pigs.body: __TODO_ES__ Pigs are the hardest livestock to load under stress. Practice repeatedly with familiar handlers, food rewards, and a short ramp. Plan water at 3 to 5 gallons per finishing pig per day.

shelter_in_place.heading: __TODO_ES__ When you cannot get out — shelter-in-place for animals
shelter_in_place.critical_body: __TODO_ES__ If you cannot evacuate animals safely, your own life takes priority. The protocol below is for animals being left behind, not for any human deciding to stay.
shelter_in_place.body: __TODO_ES__ Open all gates so animals can move away from approaching fire. Remove halters, leads, ropes, and any tie-out — entanglement kills more sheltered animals than smoke does. Move animals to the most defensible cleared area on the property — not a barn. Barns ignite and trap. Provide water in non-combustible containers — metal troughs, not plastic tubs that melt. Mark each animal with livestock paint listing your name and phone. A phone number is what reunifies after the fact. Leave a note on the gate for first responders: animals on property, count, type, owner contact.

destinations.heading: __TODO_ES__ Pre-identified evacuation destinations
destinations.required_body: __TODO_ES__ Activation of these sites is decided by San Diego County Animal Services and CAL FIRE during an active fire. Call 211 or check Genasys Protect / AlertSanDiego for which sites are open during the incident.
destinations.tip_body: __TODO_ES__ Sites that require Animal Care Check Lists or Emergency Evacuation Information packages can lose 15 to 30 minutes per arriving trailer to paperwork that could have been done at the kitchen table. Pre-fill what you can.
destinations.site_djlep_notes: __TODO_ES__ Primary horse evacuation site for east-county fires.
destinations.site_lakeside_notes: __TODO_ES__ Horses and small livestock; activated by SD County Animal Services. Operated by El Capitan Stadium Association (501(c)(3)).
destinations.site_iron_oak_notes: __TODO_ES__ Non-equine large livestock during major county events.
destinations.site_delmar_notes: __TODO_ES__ Largest capacity in the county (approximately 1,600 horses plus other large animals). Activated for major regional events.
destinations.site_sdhs_name: San Diego Humane Society Emergency Response
destinations.site_sdhs_notes: __TODO_ES__ Animal evacuation assistance for both small and large animals. Mutual aid partner of County Animal Services.
destinations.info_line_notes: __TODO_ES__ Current evacuation status, activated shelter sites, and animal services referrals during an active fire.

trailer_practice.heading: __TODO_ES__ Trailer practice calendar
trailer_practice.body: __TODO_ES__ Quarterly minimum. Rotate practice conditions: rain practice, heat practice, late-evening practice (because evacuations often hit at night). Multi-animal practice if you have multiple animals. Practice with the trailer partially loaded — hay, water, feed — so the actual evacuation isn't the first time the trailer is heavy.

insurance.heading: __TODO_ES__ Insurance and identification documentation
insurance.body: __TODO_ES__ Take photos of every animal, including distinguishing markings, color, scars, brands. Refresh annually. Maintain veterinary records — vaccination history, microchip number, Coggins for horses. Record brand inscriptions, microchip numbers, and tattoos. Keep copies in three places: cloud storage so you can access from any device, printed in your home file, printed in the go-kit.

hay_storage.heading: __TODO_ES__ Hay storage safety
hay_storage.body: __TODO_ES__ Hay is one of the highest-fuel-load items on most rural properties. A hay-barn ignition can sustain a structure fire for hours. Store hay separate from livestock barns — if a hay barn ignites, animals shouldn't be in adjacent structures. Maintain at least 100 ft of distance from the main residence, more on slopes. Use ember-resistant covering — metal roof, no exposed soft sides. Don't stack hay against any building wall.

water_dual_use.heading: __TODO_ES__ Water sources that double for firefighting
water_dual_use.body: __TODO_ES__ Pools, large tanks, and stock troughs can serve as draft sources for fire engines. Mark them clearly for crews. Standpipes or hydrants on private property: paint and label visibly. A storage tank with a separate animal supply line is a defense asset and an animal asset simultaneously. Keep the approach to any water source clear and graded for a Type 3 engine.

trigger.heading: __TODO_ES__ When to leave with animals — the trigger
trigger.critical_title: __TODO_ES__ Leave during the watch, not the warning, and never the order
trigger.critical_body: __TODO_ES__ By the time an evacuation order is issued, traffic, smoke, and panic make trailer movement dangerous. Pre-position. Hitch the trailer. Load the kit. Pack the documents. If a Red Flag Warning is issued for your zone in Santa Ana conditions, treat it as the cue to start — do not wait.

checklist.heading: __TODO_ES__ Printable animals pre-fire checklist
checklist.lead: __TODO_ES__ Use this card as a fire-season readiness check. The browser print function produces a clean printable version.

evacuate.title: __TODO_ES__ Evacuate
evacuate.description: __TODO_ES__ The Set, Go, and Return phases — routes, trigger points, alerts and apps, PSPS, smoke and air quality, and re-entry safety.

intro.critical_title: __TODO_ES__ Leave during the watch, not the warning
intro.critical_body: __TODO_ES__ Leave during the watch, not the warning, and never the order. By the time an Evacuation Order is in effect for your zone, traffic, smoke, and panic make every decision harder. The earlier you go, the safer everyone is — including the firefighters who don't have to navigate around evacuating residents.

framework.heading: __TODO_ES__ The Ready–Set–Go framework on the day of a fire
framework.body: __TODO_ES__ The framework that runs through this whole resource has a specific role on an active-fire day. Ready is the work you've already done — defensible space, home hardening, animal preparedness. Set, Go, and Return are the three phases this page covers in detail.

set_phase.heading: __TODO_ES__ Set phase — warning issued
set_phase.body: __TODO_ES__ Set means an Evacuation Warning is in effect for your zone, conditions look like an order is coming, or a Red Flag Warning is active during Santa Ana conditions. Confirm the go-bag is in the vehicle. Confirm the vehicle has at least half a tank. Apply identification to animals — collars, paint marks for livestock, leg bands. Check on neighbors with limited mobility. Hitch the trailer if you have animals. Wear protective clothing: long pants, long-sleeve shirt (cotton is best), heavy shoes, cap, dry bandanna for face cover, eye protection.

go_phase.heading: __TODO_ES__ Go phase — order issued
go_phase.body: __TODO_ES__ Go means an Evacuation Order is in effect for your zone. Leave immediately on the route already in your plan. Don't try to grab last-minute items beyond the kit. Bring neighbors who don't have transportation. Drive with headlights on, inside-air recirculation on, windows up. Don't pass on narrow roads in low visibility. Stay on pavement — fire roads are for firefighters, not evacuees. Once you're moving, stay calm and keep moving.

return_phase.heading: __TODO_ES__ Return phase — re-entry
return_phase.body: __TODO_ES__ Return means authorities have lifted the evacuation order for your zone. Re-entry hazards include charred trees that may fall, weakened power poles, hot debris, downed power lines (assume all are energized), and ash that hides deep heat for days. Wear long pants, long-sleeve shirt, gloves, work boots, and an N95 or P100 respirator while inspecting and cleaning. Document property damage with photographs before disturbing anything. Contact your insurance. Avoid opening interior doors that feel hot. Watch for sparks and embers in mulch, woodpiles, attic spaces, and under decks for at least 72 hours after the fire passes.

routes.heading: __TODO_ES__ Routes from Ranchita
routes.tip_title: __TODO_ES__ Live routing wins over planned routing
routes.tip_body: __TODO_ES__ Specific evacuation routes for an active fire are set by CAL FIRE and the San Diego County Sheriff in real time based on where the fire is. Genasys Protect and AlertSanDiego give the current direction. The framework below is what to understand in advance.
routes.body: __TODO_ES__ Ranchita's primary egress is the S22 corridor (Montezuma Valley Road), with the option of going west toward Julian and Warner Springs or east toward Borrego Springs. Authorities make the call on which direction is open during an active incident — plan to know both. Have a vehicle that can handle either drive on a full tank. If the primary corridor is blocked in both directions and authorities are routing residents through alternates, follow the official routing — do not improvise across unimproved back-routes. Vehicles get stuck on unmaintained roads. Stuck vehicles in evacuation zones become life-threatening situations.

triggers.heading: __TODO_ES__ Trigger points — when to leave early
triggers.required_title: __TODO_ES__ Red Flag plus Santa Ana = readiness state
triggers.required_body: __TODO_ES__ If a Red Flag Warning is in effect for your zone in Santa Ana wind conditions, treat it as the cue to be ready to leave on short notice.
triggers.body: __TODO_ES__ Leave during the watch, not the warning. Reasons to leave early without waiting for an order include any one of: visible smoke from your property in the direction of approaching wind; a Red Flag Warning combined with active fire activity in neighboring zones; loss of cell service or power during a Santa Ana event (information starts going dark before the fire arrives); a household member with mobility limitations or medical needs that take time to move; multiple animals that take time to load. Early evacuation is always the safer choice. Late evacuation risks include traffic, smoke that drops visibility to feet, embers landing on the vehicle, and panic at intersections.

trapped.heading: __TODO_ES__ What to do if you cannot evacuate
trapped.critical_body: __TODO_ES__ If you cannot evacuate, the goal is to survive the fire front passing. The fire front is loud, hot, and brief. Plan to outlast it, not fight it.
trapped.vehicle_body: __TODO_ES__ Park on pavement away from vegetation — outside turn if on a hillside, open lot if available. Turn headlights and emergency flashers on so other vehicles and crews can see you in heavy smoke. Close all windows and doors, shut off air vents, turn off air conditioning. Get below windows, on the floor, under wool blankets if you have them. Call 911 with your location. Stay in the vehicle — do not run. The vehicle is a survival capsule made of glass and steel; running on foot exposes you directly to flame, embers, and radiant heat. Stay until the fire front passes and outside temperatures drop. Then move to an already-burned area where there is no fuel left to burn.
trapped.home_body: __TODO_ES__ Stay inside. Close all exterior doors and windows but leave them unlocked so crews can enter if needed. Move flammable materials — patio furniture, doormats, woodpiles — at least 30 feet from the house. Shut off natural gas and propane. Open gates so animals can move and so fences don't conduct fire to the house. Fill bathtubs, sinks, garbage cans, and pools with water for post-front spot fires. Strategically position sprinklers if water pressure allows. Move to a room with the fewest exterior walls and the smallest windows, away from any direction the fire is approaching from. Stay until the fire front passes. Then inspect the structure, roof, attic, deck, and surrounding ground for embers and small fires for at least the next several hours.
trapped.foot_heading: __TODO_ES__ If on foot in the open
trapped.foot_body: __TODO_ES__ Find an already-burned area. The most dangerous place to be is in unburned fuel. If no burned area is available, find a low spot, lie face-down, cover yourself with anything non-flammable (dirt is fine), protect your airway with a cloth and your eyes with anything available. Wait for the fire front to pass. Embers and radiant heat are the killers; lying low gets you below the worst of both.

apps.heading: __TODO_ES__ Apps and alerts
apps.lead: __TODO_ES__ Install before fire season. A silent app is a useless app — test notifications after install.
apps.genasys_body: __TODO_ES__ Official San Diego County evacuation zone app. Look up your address to learn your zone code. During an evacuation order, the order references zone codes (example format: SDC-1391). Knowing your zone code in advance is how you know whether an order applies to you.
apps.alert_body: __TODO_ES__ Opt-in phone, email, and text alerts from the County. Register at alertsandiego.org. Cell broadcasts reach all phones in an active alert area without registration, but registration captures landlines and email.
apps.sdge_body: __TODO_ES__ Public Safety Power Shutoff (PSPS) and planned-outage notifications. Available as a mobile app and via SMS/email. Backcountry residents are nearly always in PSPS-eligible zones.
apps.watchduty_body: __TODO_ES__ Volunteer-maintained live wildfire tracking, independent of government delays. Useful for situational awareness when an incident is reported but not yet on official county channels.
apps.pulsepoint_body: __TODO_ES__ Real-time fire-department dispatch data. Tells you when units roll on incidents near you.
apps.airnow_body: __TODO_ES__ EPA air quality monitoring. AQI by zip code. Useful before, during, and after fires for smoke exposure decisions.

psps.heading: __TODO_ES__ Public Safety Power Shutoffs (PSPS)
psps.lead: __TODO_ES__ PSPS events are SDG&E proactively turning off power in high-fire-risk weather, primarily Santa Ana wind events in dry vegetation conditions. Backcountry circuits are the most likely to be de-energized.
psps.cadence_body: __TODO_ES__ When conditions allow, SDG&E notifies affected customers in advance: roughly 2 days ahead, 24 hours, 12 hours, 1 to 4 hours, at shutoff, and during restoration. Restoration is not instant — power lines must be patrolled, sometimes by air, before re-energizing. Daylight constraints can extend outage duration overnight.
psps.what_to_do_body: __TODO_ES__ Keep cell phones, laptops, and any medical-baseline devices charged when an event is forecast. Have a battery- or hand-cranked radio for updates. Keep refrigerator and freezer closed — food lasts roughly 4 hours in a fridge and 24 to 48 hours in a full freezer. Generators: know the safety guidelines, never run a generator indoors or in an attached garage, never connect to house wiring without a transfer switch. SDG&E Community Resource Centers open in affected communities with Wi-Fi, phone and medical-device charging, ice, water, snacks, and seasonal supplies — locations published at sdge.com/ready during active events. 211 San Diego provides enhanced PSPS support for medical-baseline customers — accessible transportation, hotel vouchers, back-up power. Free, 24/7, multi-language. Customer service at SDG&E is 800-411-7343. Downed power lines: 911.

smoke.heading: __TODO_ES__ Smoke and air quality
smoke.tip_title: __TODO_ES__ AirNow.gov gives real-time AQI by zip code
smoke.tip_body: __TODO_ES__ Smoke can be dangerous well outside the fire's visible footprint. Check AQI before any decision about going outside.
smoke.body: __TODO_ES__ Wildfire smoke is a mix of fine particulates (PM2.5) and gases. Particulates are the main health threat — they bypass the body's natural defenses and reach deep into the lungs. AQI categories: Good (0 to 50, no special precautions), Moderate (51 to 100, sensitive groups consider limiting outdoor exertion), Unhealthy for Sensitive Groups (101 to 150), Unhealthy (151 to 200), Very Unhealthy (201 to 300), Hazardous (301 and above).
smoke.indoor_body: __TODO_ES__ Keep windows and doors closed. Set HVAC to recirculate, not pull from outside. Run a portable HEPA air cleaner if available. Replace filters with MERV 13 or better where compatible. Designate one room as the cleanest-air room and seal gaps if AQI rises.
smoke.masks_body: __TODO_ES__ NIOSH-approved N95 or P100 respirators give the best protection. KN95 is acceptable. Cloth masks, surgical masks, and bandannas do not filter PM2.5 effectively. Fit matters — a respirator with a poor seal loses most of its benefit. Beards under the seal line break the seal.
smoke.vulnerable_body: __TODO_ES__ Anyone with asthma, COPD, heart disease, pregnancy, young children, and the elderly should be more cautious. Symptoms that warrant medical attention include severe cough, shortness of breath, chest pain, dizziness, and confusion.

reentry.heading: __TODO_ES__ Re-entry safety
reentry.lead_body: __TODO_ES__ Re-entry is when the fire has moved on but the hazards have not. Many fire-related injuries happen in the days after the front passes.
reentry.body: __TODO_ES__ Do not return until authorities lift the evacuation order. The area looks calm but ground heat persists in ash and root systems for days. Charred trees can fall without warning. Weakened power poles and downed lines must be assumed energized. Hot embers in mulch, woodpiles, and attic spaces can flare for 72 hours or more.
reentry.ppe_body: __TODO_ES__ Long pants, long-sleeve shirt, gloves, work boots, N95 or P100 respirator. Document damage with photographs before moving anything. Contact insurance. Inspect the roof, attic, decks, fences, propane fittings. Check water lines for damage. If gas service was on during the fire, check with the utility before relighting pilots — don't light a match, candle, or cigarette anywhere on the property until you've cleared this with the gas utility.

checklist.heading: __TODO_ES__ Printable evacuate-phase checklist
checklist.lead: __TODO_ES__ The browser print function produces a clean printable version of the checklist below.

mutual_aid.title: __TODO_ES__ Mutual Aid
mutual_aid.description: __TODO_ES__ How we show up for each other in Ranchita — and stay out of the way of the people fighting fires. Year-round community work, neighbor check-in lists, communications, and the line between help and interference.

intro.para_1: __TODO_ES__ Ranchita's geography means professional response is volunteer-supported and farther away than in town. The closest crews are minutes — sometimes longer — from any address in the valley, and the people first on a scene are almost always neighbors.
intro.para_2: __TODO_ES__ The way we look out for each other here isn't a workaround. It's part of how this place actually functions, year-round, not just during fires. The work that follows on this page is what neighbor-helping-neighbor looks like in practice — before, during, and after.

support.heading: __TODO_ES__ How residents support firefighters
support.critical_body: __TODO_ES__ Show up for your neighbors. Stay out of the way of the firefighters. The line between those two is the discipline this section is about.
support.body: __TODO_ES__ Residents are first on most fire scenes by definition of geography. Useful resident actions during an incident include wetting down your own property, wetting down a neighbor's property if you can do it safely, moving animals, checking on elderly neighbors, clearing your driveway for engine access. Useful resident actions that do NOT happen near the fire line include anything that puts you in the same airspace, water source, or road segment as professional crews. Crews work to a plan that you don't have visibility into. Standing in their water source, blocking their road segment, or asking them questions during an active push wastes time they don't have.

year_round.heading: __TODO_ES__ Year-round community work
year_round.lead: __TODO_ES__ The work that matters most happens months before any fire.
year_round.roads_body: __TODO_ES__ The community-scale extension of the Zone 3 access principle. Branches that hang into S22 or feeder roads, brush that encroaches on the shoulders, gate-side vegetation that blocks engine access — these are the things neighbors clearing together on a Saturday morning in spring address before fire season. Coordinate via existing community channels.
year_round.chipping_body: __TODO_ES__ Defensible-space work generates piles of brush, branches, and slash. A community chipping day rents a chipper for a weekend, neighbors rotate through, and the cleared material gets reduced to mulch (which, ironically, gets used in Zone 2, not Zone 0). Spring is the right window. Cost-share by parcel.
year_round.water_body: __TODO_ES__ The community knows where the water is — pools, large tanks, stock troughs that fire crews could draft from. Compile that map. Update it annually as properties change. Share it with the local fire authority through proper channels — the local San Diego County Fire station, not a one-off conversation with an engine crew during a fire. Marked water sources visible from the driveway help crews who don't know your property.
year_round.driveway_body: __TODO_ES__ A driveway that can't accept a Type 3 engine isn't useful in a fire — it's a liability. The community can help each other with gate hinge orientation, overhead clearance trims, address-number reflective signage, and turnaround space at the back of long driveways. The work of one weekend on a property whose owner can't physically do it themselves is the most concrete form of mutual aid available.

checkin.heading: __TODO_ES__ Neighbor check-in lists
checkin.lead_body: __TODO_ES__ The people most at risk in an evacuation are the ones who need help and don't ask. The community list is how that gets addressed.
checkin.body: __TODO_ES__ Identify, with permission and respectfully, the neighbors who would benefit from a check-in if a Red Flag Warning hits. Categories that often need help include elderly residents living alone, residents with mobility limitations, residents on home medical equipment dependent on power, single-parent households with small children, households with many animals to load, residents without transportation, and residents who travel for work and may be away when an order hits. The list lives somewhere multiple neighbors can access — not on one person's phone. Update at least annually. Privacy matters: this list contains sensitive personal information and should be treated accordingly.

comms.heading: __TODO_ES__ Communications during incidents
comms.tip_title: __TODO_ES__ Cell service is not reliable here in Santa Anas
comms.tip_body: __TODO_ES__ Cell service in Ranchita is not reliable during Santa Ana wind events. Plan for the day cell goes down.
comms.lead: __TODO_ES__ Redundancy is the answer. Cell phones first, but assume they will be unreliable.
comms.phone_tree_body: __TODO_ES__ A predetermined order of who calls whom. Five neighbors in a tree, each calling one other neighbor and so on, gets information moving without overloading any one person. Update when residents move in or out.
comms.frs_body: __TODO_ES__ Family Radio Service handhelds (no license required) and General Mobile Radio Service (license required for transmit, one license covers a household) give a backup that works when cell does not. A common channel agreed in advance lets neighbors check in across the valley without infrastructure. Range varies with terrain — line-of-sight is best.
comms.ham_body: __TODO_ES__ Amateur radio is the most resilient option for genuine grid-down conditions. Licensing takes a weekend course and an exam. For communities with one or two licensed operators, the rest of the community gets reach beyond what FRS or GMRS provides. Local clubs can advise.
comms.rally_body: __TODO_ES__ A specific physical location everyone in the community knows as the place to check in if comms are down. Outside the immediate fire path, on the egress route, with parking. This is not the evacuation site itself — it's the local confirmation-of-status point before people move on.

line.heading: __TODO_ES__ When help is help — and when it's interference
line.never_body: __TODO_ES__ Never insert yourself between firefighters and the fire. Never use a water source crews are drafting from. Never enter an evacuated zone to retrieve property.
line.body: __TODO_ES__ The difference between mutual aid and getting in the way is mostly about staying in your own role. Your role is your property, your neighbors' property at their request, and the people in your community who need help moving. The fire line is not your role. Residents who try to defend against an active fire front directly, without training and equipment, become casualties — and become a search-and-rescue burden that pulls firefighters off the actual fire. The most useful thing a resident can do during the active push is to have already evacuated, with their neighbors, with their animals, on the route the authorities are routing to. The most useful thing during pre-fire conditions is the year-round work above.

after.heading: __TODO_ES__ After the fire — rebuilding together
after.lead: __TODO_ES__ The recovery is longer than the fire. The community's work is most needed in the weeks and months after.
after.body: __TODO_ES__ Neighbors help each other navigate insurance claims, document photographs, debris removal, and rebuilding permits. The county opens recovery resources after major events; a community that coordinates the application of those resources gets more benefit than households navigating individually.
after.emotional_body: __TODO_ES__ Post-fire is a long mental-health tail. Residents who lost homes, who lost animals, who watched the fire come close, who evacuated multiple times in one season — these are the people who need community presence in the months after the smoke clears. The check-in list stays active well past the all-clear.

checklist.heading: __TODO_ES__ Printable mutual-aid checklist
checklist.lead: __TODO_ES__ The browser print function produces a clean printable version of the checklist below.

neighbor_ethos.heading: __TODO_ES__ Neighbors helping neighbors
neighbor_ethos.body: __TODO_ES__ In Ranchita, help is far away. The closest professional firefighters are minutes — sometimes longer — from any address in the valley, and the people first on a scene are almost always neighbors. That's not a failing of the system; it's the geography. The way we look out for each other here is part of the system. We wet down a neighbor's roof when they're at work two hours away. We move livestock when the smoke shifts. We check on the elderly couple at the end of the road before we leave for our own evacuation. And — just as importantly — we know when to step back, so the firefighters and the trained crews who arrive can do their job without us in the way. Showing up for each other doesn't mean playing fire department. It means being ready, being aware, and knowing the line.

risk.heading: __TODO_ES__ Local risk profile
risk.topography_para: __TODO_ES__ S22 places Ranchita at a ridgeline where prevailing winds and terrain can drive fire downhill toward Borrego Springs in one direction and through canyons toward the western mountain communities in the other. The single S22 corridor is the primary egress for most addresses; alternates exist but are slower and require local knowledge.
risk.fuels_para: __TODO_ES__ Dominant fuels are mature chaparral (manzanita, ceanothus, chamise) with pockets of oak and pine. Chaparral is one of the most ember-productive fuel types in California — embers can travel a mile or more downwind ahead of a fire front, igniting structures via vents, debris, and combustible landscaping long before the flame arrives.
risk.winds_para: __TODO_ES__ Santa Ana wind events from late summer through winter are the highest-risk periods, but drought-year fuel moisture means a wind event in any season can produce extreme fire behavior. Plan year-round.
risk.response_para: __TODO_ES__ Backcountry response is volunteer-supported. Mutual aid arrives, but with travel time. The first thirty minutes of any fire belong to whoever is on scene — usually you and your neighbors.

contacts.heading: __TODO_ES__ Emergency contacts
contacts.subhead: __TODO_ES__ Save these to your phone now. Cell service is unreliable during fires.
contacts.info_note: __TODO_ES__ Current evacuation status, shelter availability, animal services referrals.
contacts.genasys_note: __TODO_ES__ Official SD County evacuation zone app. Look up your zone before fire season.
contacts.alert_note: __TODO_ES__ Opt-in phone, email, and text alerts from the County.

not_replacement.title: __TODO_ES__ This guide does not replace official guidance
not_replacement.body: __TODO_ES__ Read CAL FIRE's Ready for Wildfire program and San Diego County Fire's Ready, Set, Go! Wildland Fire Action Guide directly. This page extends them with Ranchita-specific layers — it does not substitute for either.

draft_notice.title: __TODO_ES__ Active draft
draft_notice.body: __TODO_ES__ This resource is in active draft. MVVCSO is refining content with the local board. Some sections will continue to evolve as board members and longtime community members layer in their firsthand experience.

phases.ready_summary: __TODO_ES__ Defensible space, home hardening, animal preparedness — done before there is a fire.
phases.set_summary: __TODO_ES__ Watch is issued. Pre-position. Stay alert.
phases.go_summary: __TODO_ES__ Evacuation order. Leave on the route you've already chosen.
phases.return_summary: __TODO_ES__ Re-entry hazards: power lines, weakened trees, hot debris.
